End-of-Day Revision Card — Day 9

10-point summary

  1. Revise the definition and purpose of Data sufficiency.
  2. Write formulas once by hand before attempting speed questions.
  3. Solve easy questions first to build accuracy.
  4. Use option elimination wherever full calculation is long.
  5. Track every mistake in the weak-area dashboard.
  6. Reattempt wrong quiz questions without seeing the answer.
  7. Mark completed topics only after solving examples.
  8. Keep verbal practice short but consistent.
  9. Use shortcuts only after understanding the concept.
  10. End the day by reviewing tomorrow’s topic list.

Formula recap

  • Ranking: Top + Bottom = Total + 1 — Use for rank from opposite side.
  • Direction: Cancel opposite directions — Draw a small compass.
  • Ages: Future = present + years — Use timeline.

Tricks recap

  • Data sufficiency: Before solving Data sufficiency, identify the exact concept from the wording; the first correct identification saves most time.
  • Ranking: Before solving Ranking, identify the exact concept from the wording; the first correct identification saves most time.
  • Direction sense: Before solving Direction sense, identify the exact concept from the wording; the first correct identification saves most time.
  • Ages: Before solving Ages, identify the exact concept from the wording; the first correct identification saves most time.

Mistake checklist

  • Wrong base value
  • Wrong unit conversion
  • Misread option
  • Skipped condition
  • No revision of incorrect answers

Weak-area revision advice: Any topic below 60% must be revised before moving to hard questions.

Tomorrow’s preparation preview: Day 10 — Seating, Blood Relations, Puzzles.

Day 10 • 5 June 2026

Day 10 — Seating, Blood Relations, Puzzles

Study objective: Build practical command over Seating arrangement, Linear arrangement, Circular arrangement, Blood relations and apply it in TCS NQT-style timed questions.

Previous DayNext Day
Day mini TOC: Seating arrangement Linear arrangement Circular arrangement Blood relations Family tree Puzzle basics PracticeQuizScoreRevision

Topics covered

  • Seating arrangement
  • Linear arrangement
  • Circular arrangement
  • Blood relations
  • Family tree
  • Puzzle basics
Seating arrangement

Seating arrangement needs structured placement. Draw slots for linear arrangement, circles for circular arrangement, and family-tree arrows for blood relations. Place definite information first, then handle conditions.

  1. Step 1: Read the question and identify that it belongs to Seating arrangement.
  2. Step 2: Write the given values or conditions in short form.
  3. Step 3: Choose the correct rule, formula, or logic pattern.
  4. Step 4: Solve neatly and compare with options.
  5. Step 5: Recheck units, signs, and hidden conditions before final answer.

Tricks to Remember

Concept trick

Before solving Seating arrangement, identify the exact concept from the wording; the first correct identification saves most time.

Formula shortcut

Write only the necessary formula for Seating arrangement; avoid using long methods when an option-based shortcut works.

Memory method

Remember Fixed-position trick as the first shortcut for this topic.

Pattern recognition trick

Look for repeated structures, ratios, signs, keywords, and option patterns in Seating arrangement.

Option elimination trick

Eliminate impossible options using parity, magnitude, grammar sense, or boundary values.

Common trap

The usual trap in Seating arrangement is using the wrong base, wrong unit, wrong order, or assuming extra information.

30-second solving method

Convert the question into one line of formula or one small diagram, then test the closest option.

Fast solving method for TCS NQT

Use Fixed-position trick, Family tree arrow trick and avoid lengthy manual expansion.

Solved Examples

Example 1: Find next: 3, 7, 11, 15, ?
Difference is +4, so next = 19.
Shortcut: Check difference first.
Example 2: A>B and B>C. Who is tallest?
A is tallest because A>B>C.
Shortcut: Write comparison chain.

Common Mistakes

  • Starting Seating arrangement without identifying the exact concept.
  • Copying numbers incorrectly from the question.
  • Selecting the first matching option without checking all conditions.
  • Ignoring units, signs, order, tense, or base value.
  • Not reviewing wrong answers after practice.
Linear arrangement

Linear arrangement needs structured placement. Draw slots for linear arrangement, circles for circular arrangement, and family-tree arrows for blood relations. Place definite information first, then handle conditions.

  1. Step 1: Read the question and identify that it belongs to Linear arrangement.
  2. Step 2: Write the given values or conditions in short form.
  3. Step 3: Choose the correct rule, formula, or logic pattern.
  4. Step 4: Solve neatly and compare with options.
  5. Step 5: Recheck units, signs, and hidden conditions before final answer.

Tricks to Remember

Concept trick

Before solving Linear arrangement, identify the exact concept from the wording; the first correct identification saves most time.

Formula shortcut

Write only the necessary formula for Linear arrangement; avoid using long methods when an option-based shortcut works.

Memory method

Remember Fixed-position trick as the first shortcut for this topic.

Pattern recognition trick

Look for repeated structures, ratios, signs, keywords, and option patterns in Linear arrangement.

Option elimination trick

Eliminate impossible options using parity, magnitude, grammar sense, or boundary values.

Common trap

The usual trap in Linear arrangement is using the wrong base, wrong unit, wrong order, or assuming extra information.

30-second solving method

Convert the question into one line of formula or one small diagram, then test the closest option.

Fast solving method for TCS NQT

Use Fixed-position trick, Family tree arrow trick and avoid lengthy manual expansion.

Solved Examples

Example 1: Find next: 3, 7, 11, 15, ?
Difference is +4, so next = 19.
Shortcut: Check difference first.
Example 2: A>B and B>C. Who is tallest?
A is tallest because A>B>C.
Shortcut: Write comparison chain.

Common Mistakes

  • Starting Linear arrangement without identifying the exact concept.
  • Copying numbers incorrectly from the question.
  • Selecting the first matching option without checking all conditions.
  • Ignoring units, signs, order, tense, or base value.
  • Not reviewing wrong answers after practice.
Circular arrangement

Circular arrangement needs structured placement. Draw slots for linear arrangement, circles for circular arrangement, and family-tree arrows for blood relations. Place definite information first, then handle conditions.

  1. Step 1: Read the question and identify that it belongs to Circular arrangement.
  2. Step 2: Write the given values or conditions in short form.
  3. Step 3: Choose the correct rule, formula, or logic pattern.
  4. Step 4: Solve neatly and compare with options.
  5. Step 5: Recheck units, signs, and hidden conditions before final answer.

Important Formulas and Rules

Formula/RuleExpressionWhen to use
Linear seatingUse slotsMark facing direction.
Circular seatingFix one personReduces rotation confusion.
Blood relationDecode inside outwardConvert each phrase slowly.

Practice Question Bank — 65 Questions

All questions below are original TCS NQT PYQ-style or concept-practice questions with answers and explanations.

Q1. Pointing to a boy, Neha says, 'He is the son of my father's only son.' How is the boy related to Neha?

Seating arrangementcircularMediumTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. Brother
  2. B. Uncle
  3. C. Nephew
  4. D. Father
Show answer and explanation

Answer: C. Nephew

Explanation: Father's only son is Neha's brother if Neha is female, but the son of Neha's brother is nephew. However the question says pointing to a boy and asks relation to Neha; if Neha's father's only son is Neha's brother, his son is Neha's nephew.

Shortcut: Convert each phrase from inside outward.

Q2. In a family tree, P is Q's father and Q is R's mother. How is Q related to R?

Linear arrangementblood relationMediumTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. Mother
  2. B. Aunt
  3. C. Daughter
  4. D. Sister
Show answer and explanation

Answer: A. Mother

Explanation: The statement directly says Q is R's mother.

Shortcut: Do not overcomplicate direct relation statements.

Q3. In a circular table, each person has exactly how many immediate neighbours?

Circular arrangementfamily treeHardTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. 3
  2. B. 2
  3. C. 1
  4. D. 4
Show answer and explanation

Answer: B. 2

Explanation: In a circle, one person sits on the immediate left and one on the immediate right, so there are 2 immediate neighbours.

Shortcut: Circular arrangements wrap around; first and last are neighbours.

Q4. Five people A, B, C, D, E sit in a row facing north. A is left of B, C is right of B, D is right of C, and E is left of A. Who sits in the middle?

Blood relationspuzzleEasyTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. A
  2. B. D
  3. C. B
  4. D. C
Show answer and explanation

Answer: C. B

Explanation: Arrange step by step: E is left of A, A is left of B, C is right of B, D is right of C. Order is E-A-B-C-D. The middle person is B.

Shortcut: For linear seating facing north, left and right remain as normal.

Q5. Pointing to a boy, Neha says, 'He is the son of my father's only son.' How is the boy related to Neha?

  3. C. Nephew
  4. D. Father

Q39. In a circular table, each person has exactly how many immediate neighbours?

Circular arrangementpuzzleHardTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. 3
  2. B. 2
  3. C. 1
  4. D. 4
Show answer and explanation

Answer: B. 2

Explanation: In a circle, one person sits on the immediate left and one on the immediate right, so there are 2 immediate neighbours.

Shortcut: Circular arrangements wrap around; first and last are neighbours.

Q40. Five people A, B, C, D, E sit in a row facing north. A is left of B, C is right of B, D is right of C, and E is left of A. Who sits in the middle?

Blood relationslinearEasyConcept Practice
  1. A. A
  2. B. D
  3. C. B
  4. D. C
Show answer and explanation

Answer: C. B

Explanation: Arrange step by step: E is left of A, A is left of B, C is right of B, D is right of C. Order is E-A-B-C-D. The middle person is B.

Shortcut: For linear seating facing north, left and right remain as normal.

Q41. Pointing to a boy, Neha says, 'He is the son of my father's only son.' How is the boy related to Neha?

Family treecircularMediumTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. Brother
  2. B. Uncle
  3. C. Nephew
  4. D. Father
Show answer and explanation

Answer: C. Nephew

Explanation: Father's only son is Neha's brother if Neha is female, but the son of Neha's brother is nephew. However the question says pointing to a boy and asks relation to Neha; if Neha's father's only son is Neha's brother, his son is Neha's nephew.

Shortcut: Convert each phrase from inside outward.

Q42. In a family tree, P is Q's father and Q is R's mother. How is Q related to R?

Puzzle basicsblood relationMediumTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. Mother
  2. B. Aunt
  3. C. Daughter
  4. D. Sister
Show answer and explanation

Answer: A. Mother

Explanation: The statement directly says Q is R's mother.

Shortcut: Do not overcomplicate direct relation statements.

Select answers and submit to update LocalStorage score, accuracy, topic-wise score, and weak areas.

End-of-Day Revision Card — Day 10

10-point summary

  1. Revise the definition and purpose of Seating arrangement.
  2. Write formulas once by hand before attempting speed questions.
  3. Solve easy questions first to build accuracy.
  4. Use option elimination wherever full calculation is long.
  5. Track every mistake in the weak-area dashboard.
  6. Reattempt wrong quiz questions without seeing the answer.
  7. Mark completed topics only after solving examples.
  8. Keep verbal practice short but consistent.
  9. Use shortcuts only after understanding the concept.
  10. End the day by reviewing tomorrow’s topic list.

Formula recap

  • Linear seating: Use slots — Mark facing direction.
  • Circular seating: Fix one person — Reduces rotation confusion.
  • Blood relation: Decode inside outward — Convert each phrase slowly.

Tricks recap

  • Seating arrangement: Before solving Seating arrangement, identify the exact concept from the wording; the first correct identification saves most time.
  • Linear arrangement: Before solving Linear arrangement, identify the exact concept from the wording; the first correct identification saves most time.
  • Circular arrangement: Before solving Circular arrangement, identify the exact concept from the wording; the first correct identification saves most time.
  • Blood relations: Before solving Blood relations, identify the exact concept from the wording; the first correct identification saves most time.

Mistake checklist

  • Wrong base value
  • Wrong unit conversion
  • Misread option
  • Skipped condition
  • No revision of incorrect answers

Weak-area revision advice: Any topic below 60% must be revised before moving to hard questions.

Tomorrow’s preparation preview: Day 11 — Coding-Decoding and Word Logic.

Day 11 • 6 June 2026

Day 11 — Coding-Decoding and Word Logic

Study objective: Build practical command over Coding-decoding, Letter coding, Number coding, Symbol coding and apply it in TCS NQT-style timed questions.

Previous DayNext Day
Day mini TOC: Coding-decoding Letter coding Number coding Symbol coding Meaningful word formation Word arrangement Dictionary order PracticeQuizScoreRevision

Topics covered

  • Coding-decoding
  • Letter coding
  • Number coding
  • Symbol coding
  • Meaningful word formation
  • Word arrangement
  • Dictionary order
Coding-decoding

Coding-decoding builds programming logic for TCS NQT coding and MCQ rounds. Focus on dry runs, variable updates, loop boundaries, array indexing, string traversal, and base conditions in recursion.

  1. Step 1: Read the question and identify that it belongs to Coding-decoding.
  2. Step 2: Write the given values or conditions in short form.
  3. Step 3: Choose the correct rule, formula, or logic pattern.
  4. Step 4: Solve neatly and compare with options.
  5. Step 5: Recheck units, signs, and hidden conditions before final answer.

Tricks to Remember

Concept trick

Before solving Coding-decoding, identify the exact concept from the wording; the first correct identification saves most time.

Formula shortcut

Write only the necessary formula for Coding-decoding; avoid using long methods when an option-based shortcut works.

Memory method

Remember Dry run trick as the first shortcut for this topic.

Pattern recognition trick

Look for repeated structures, ratios, signs, keywords, and option patterns in Coding-decoding.

Option elimination trick

Eliminate impossible options using parity, magnitude, grammar sense, or boundary values.

Common trap

The usual trap in Coding-decoding is using the wrong base, wrong unit, wrong order, or assuming extra information.

30-second solving method

Convert the question into one line of formula or one small diagram, then test the closest option.

Fast solving method for TCS NQT

Use Dry run trick, Boundary check trick and avoid lengthy manual expansion.

Solved Examples

Example 1: for i=1 to 3, add i to sum.
sum starts 0. After i=1 sum=1, i=2 sum=3, i=3 sum=6. Output 6.
Shortcut: Make trace table.
Example 2: Check if 'madam' is palindrome.
Reverse is 'madam', same as original, so palindrome.
Shortcut: Compare both ends.

Common Mistakes

  • Starting Coding-decoding without identifying the exact concept.
  • Copying numbers incorrectly from the question.
  • Selecting the first matching option without checking all conditions.
  • Ignoring units, signs, order, tense, or base value.
  • Not reviewing wrong answers after practice.

Reverse is 'madam', same as original, so palindrome.

Meaningful word formation

Meaningful word formation requires pattern recognition. Check differences, double differences, multiplication, division, squares, cubes, alternate terms, and alphabet positions. Do not force only one pattern; test options quickly.

  1. Step 1: Read the question and identify that it belongs to Meaningful word formation.
  2. Step 2: Write the given values or conditions in short form.
  3. Step 3: Choose the correct rule, formula, or logic pattern.
  4. Step 4: Solve neatly and compare with options.
  5. Step 5: Recheck units, signs, and hidden conditions before final answer.

Tricks to Remember

Concept trick

Before solving Meaningful word formation, identify the exact concept from the wording; the first correct identification saves most time.

Formula shortcut

Write only the necessary formula for Meaningful word formation; avoid using long methods when an option-based shortcut works.

Memory method

Remember Pattern recognition trick as the first shortcut for this topic.

Pattern recognition trick

Look for repeated structures, ratios, signs, keywords, and option patterns in Meaningful word formation.

Option elimination trick

Eliminate impossible options using parity, magnitude, grammar sense, or boundary values.

Common trap

The usual trap in Meaningful word formation is using the wrong base, wrong unit, wrong order, or assuming extra information.

30-second solving method

Convert the question into one line of formula or one small diagram, then test the closest option.

Fast solving method for TCS NQT

Use Pattern recognition trick, Alternate term trick and avoid lengthy manual expansion.

Solved Examples

Example 1: Find next: 3, 7, 11, 15, ?
Difference is +4, so next = 19.
Shortcut: Check difference first.
Example 2: A>B and B>C. Who is tallest?
A is tallest because A>B>C.
Shortcut: Write comparison chain.

Common Mistakes

  • Starting Meaningful word formation without identifying the exact concept.
  • Copying numbers incorrectly from the question.
  • Selecting the first matching option without checking all conditions.
  • Ignoring units, signs, order, tense, or base value.
  • Not reviewing wrong answers after practice.
Word arrangement

Word arrangement needs structured placement. Draw slots for linear arrangement, circles for circular arrangement, and family-tree arrows for blood relations. Place definite information first, then handle conditions.

  1. Step 1: Read the question and identify that it belongs to Word arrangement.
  2. Step 2: Write the given values or conditions in short form.
  3. Step 3: Choose the correct rule, formula, or logic pattern.
  4. Step 4: Solve neatly and compare with options.
  5. Step 5: Recheck units, signs, and hidden conditions before final answer.

Tricks to Remember

Concept trick

Before solving Word arrangement, identify the exact concept from the wording; the first correct identification saves most time.

Formula shortcut

Write only the necessary formula for Word arrangement; avoid using long methods when an option-based shortcut works.

Memory method

Remember Fixed-position trick as the first shortcut for this topic.

Pattern recognition trick

Look for repeated structures, ratios, signs, keywords, and option patterns in Word arrangement.

Option elimination trick

Eliminate impossible options using parity, magnitude, grammar sense, or boundary values.

Common trap

The usual trap in Word arrangement is using the wrong base, wrong unit, wrong order, or assuming extra information.

30-second solving method

Convert the question into one line of formula or one small diagram, then test the closest option.

Fast solving method for TCS NQT

Use Fixed-position trick, Family tree arrow trick and avoid lengthy manual expansion.

Solved Examples

Example 1: Find next: 3, 7, 11, 15, ?
Difference is +4, so next = 19.
Shortcut: Check difference first.
Example 2: A>B and B>C. Who is tallest?
A is tallest because A>B>C.
Shortcut: Write comparison chain.

Common Mistakes

  • Starting Word arrangement without identifying the exact concept.
  • Copying numbers incorrectly from the question.
  • Selecting the first matching option without checking all conditions.
  • Ignoring units, signs, order, tense, or base value.
  • Not reviewing wrong answers after practice.
Dictionary order

Dictionary order requires pattern recognition. Check differences, double differences, multiplication, division, squares, cubes, alternate terms, and alphabet positions. Do not force only one pattern; test options quickly.

  1. Step 1: Read the question and identify that it belongs to Dictionary order.
  2. Step 2: Write the given values or conditions in short form.
  3. Step 3: Choose the correct rule, formula, or logic pattern.
  4. Step 4: Solve neatly and compare with options.
  5. Step 5: Recheck units, signs, and hidden conditions before final answer.

Tricks to Remember

Concept trick

Before solving Dictionary order, identify the exact concept from the wording; the first correct identification saves most time.

Formula shortcut

Write only the necessary formula for Dictionary order; avoid using long methods when an option-based shortcut works.

Memory method

Remember Pattern recognition trick as the first shortcut for this topic.

Pattern recognition trick

Look for repeated structures, ratios, signs, keywords, and option patterns in Dictionary order.

Option elimination trick

Eliminate impossible options using parity, magnitude, grammar sense, or boundary values.

Common trap

The usual trap in Dictionary order is using the wrong base, wrong unit, wrong order, or assuming extra information.

30-second solving method

Convert the question into one line of formula or one small diagram, then test the closest option.

Fast solving method for TCS NQT

Use Pattern recognition trick, Alternate term trick and avoid lengthy manual expansion.

Solved Examples

Example 1: Find next: 3, 7, 11, 15, ?
Difference is +4, so next = 19.
Shortcut: Check difference first.
Example 2: A>B and B>C. Who is tallest?
A is tallest because A>B>C.
Shortcut: Write comparison chain.

Common Mistakes

  • Starting Dictionary order without identifying the exact concept.
  • Copying numbers incorrectly from the question.
  • Selecting the first matching option without checking all conditions.
  • Ignoring units, signs, order, tense, or base value.
  • Not reviewing wrong answers after practice.

Important Formulas and Rules

Formula/RuleExpressionWhen to use
Loop dry runTrack variable after each iterationUse a small table.
Moduloa % b = remainderUseful for digit and divisibility code.
Array traversalIndex 0 to n-1Avoid out-of-bound errors.
Arithmetic seriesCheck differenceMost common first check.
Double differenceCheck change in differencesFor quadratic patterns.
Alphabet codingA=1, B=2, ..., Z=26Use positions.
Linear seatingUse slotsMark facing direction.
Circular seatingFix one personReduces rotation confusion.
Blood relationDecode inside outwardConvert each phrase slowly.

Practice Question Bank — 65 Questions

All questions below are original TCS NQT PYQ-style or concept-practice questions with answers and explanations.

Q1. What is the value of 6 % 5 in C-style programming?

Coding-decodingdebuggingMediumTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. 11
  2. B. 30
  3. C. 9
  4. D. 1
Show answer and explanation

Answer: D. 1

Explanation: The % operator gives remainder. 6 divided by 5 leaves remainder 1.

Shortcut: Modulo gives remainder, not quotient.

Q2. Given array [2, 4, 9, 5], what value will a maximum-finding loop return?

Letter codingdry runMediumTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. 4
  2. B. 20
  3. C. 2
  4. D. 9
Show answer and explanation

Answer: D. 9

Explanation: Compare each element and keep the largest. The maximum among [2, 4, 9, 5] is 9.

Shortcut: Initialize max with first element, then compare one by one.

Q3. Classify the string 'tcs' after comparing it with its reverse.

Number codingloop traceHardTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. Not palindrome
  2. B. Anagram
  3. C. Empty string
  4. D. Sorted string
Show answer and explanation

Answer: A. Not palindrome

Explanation: Reverse of 'tcs' is 'sct'. Since it does not match, it is not palindrome.

Shortcut: Palindrome check: compare first with last, second with second last.

Q4. What does a factorial function return for input 9?

Symbol codingarray/string logicEasyTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. 362889
  2. B. 362879
  3. C. 40320
  4. D. 362880
Show answer and explanation

Answer: D. 362880

Explanation: Factorial means product from 1 to 9. 9! = 362880.

Shortcut: Recursive factorial: fact(n)=n×fact(n-1), base fact(0)=1.

Q5. Find the next number in the series: 2, 8, 14, 20, 26, ?

Meaningful word formationdifferenceMediumConcept Practice
  1. A. 26
  2. B. 38
  3. C. 32
  4. D. 34
Show answer and explanation

Answer: C. 32

Explanation: The difference is constant: 6. Next term = 26 + 6 = 32.

Shortcut: First check constant difference.

Q6. In a family tree, P is Q's father and Q is R's mother. How is Q related to R?

Word arrangementcircularMediumTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. Mother
  2. B. Aunt
  3. C. Daughter
  4. D. Sister
Show answer and explanation

Answer: A. Mother

Explanation: The statement directly says Q is R's mother.

Shortcut: Do not overcomplicate direct relation statements.

Q7. If each letter is shifted forward by 3, what will C become?

Dictionary orderalphabet shiftHardTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. Z
  2. B. F
  3. C. H
  4. D. G
Show answer and explanation

Answer: B. F

Explanation: Move 3 positions ahead in the alphabet from C. The result is F.

Shortcut: Write alphabet positions A=1, B=2, ..., Z=26.

Q8. What is the value of 8 % 5 in C-style programming?

Coding-decodingloop traceEasyTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. 1
  2. B. 13
  3. C. 40
  4. D. 3
Show answer and explanation

Answer: D. 3

Explanation: The % operator gives remainder. 8 divided by 5 leaves remainder 3.

Shortcut: Modulo gives remainder, not quotient.

Letter codingarray/string logicMediumTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. 2
  2. B. 19
  3. C. 4
  4. D. 9
Show answer and explanation

Answer: D. 9

Explanation: Compare each element and keep the largest. The maximum among [2, 4, 9, 4] is 9.

Shortcut: Initialize max with first element, then compare one by one.

Q10. Classify the string 'level' after comparing it with its reverse.

Number codingoutput predictionMediumConcept Practice
  1. A. Palindrome
  2. B. Empty string
  3. C. Anagram
  4. D. Sorted string
Show answer and explanation

Answer: A. Palindrome

Explanation: Reverse of 'level' is 'level'. Since it matches, it is palindrome.

Shortcut: Palindrome check: compare first with last, second with second last.

Q11. What does a factorial function return for input 6?

Symbol codingdebuggingHardTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. 719
  2. B. 720
  3. C. 120
  4. D. 726
Show answer and explanation

Answer: B. 720

Explanation: Factorial means product from 1 to 6. 6! = 720.

Shortcut: Recursive factorial: fact(n)=n×fact(n-1), base fact(0)=1.

Q12. If each letter is shifted forward by 3, what will T become?

Meaningful word formationalphabet shiftEasyTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. W
  2. B. Y
  3. C. Q
  4. D. X
Show answer and explanation

Answer: A. W

Explanation: Move 3 positions ahead in the alphabet from T. The result is W.

Shortcut: Write alphabet positions A=1, B=2, ..., Z=26.

Q13. Pointing to a boy, Neha says, 'He is the son of my father's only son.' How is the boy related to Neha?

Word arrangementfamily treeMediumTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. Brother
  2. B. Uncle
  3. C. Nephew
  4. D. Father
Show answer and explanation

Answer: C. Nephew

Explanation: Father's only son is Neha's brother if Neha is female, but the son of Neha's brother is nephew. However the question says pointing to a boy and asks relation to Neha; if Neha's father's only son is Neha's brother, his son is Neha's nephew.

Shortcut: Convert each phrase from inside outward.

Q14. Find the odd one out: Apple, Mango, Chair, Banana.

Dictionary orderwrong numberMediumTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. Chair
  2. B. Mango
  3. C. Apple
  4. D. Banana
Show answer and explanation

Answer: A. Chair

Explanation: Apple, Mango and Banana are fruits. Chair is furniture. Therefore Chair is the odd one out.

Shortcut: Classify options by category before eliminating.

Explanation: The % operator gives remainder. 5 divided by 5 leaves remainder 0.

Answer: B. 40320

Q20. Five people A, B, C, D, E sit in a row facing north. A is left of B, C is right of B, D is right of C, and E is left of A. Who sits in the middle?

Word arrangementlinearEasyConcept Practice
  1. A. A
  2. B. D
  3. C. B
  4. D. C
Show answer and explanation

Answer: C. B

Explanation: Arrange step by step: E is left of A, A is left of B, C is right of B, D is right of C. Order is E-A-B-C-D. The middle person is B.

Shortcut: For linear seating facing north, left and right remain as normal.

Q21. Find the next number: 3, 6, 11, 18, 27, ?

Dictionary orderalternate patternMediumTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. 32
  2. B. 45
  3. C. 33
  4. D. 38
Show answer and explanation

Answer: D. 38

Explanation: Terms add square numbers: 1², 2², 3², 4², 5². Next uses 6², so answer is 2+36 = 38.

Shortcut: After simple difference, check square/cube patterns.

  3. C. 39

Q27. In a circular table, each person has exactly how many immediate neighbours?

Word arrangementblood relationHardTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. 3
  2. B. 2
  3. C. 1
  4. D. 4
Show answer and explanation

Answer: B. 2

Explanation: In a circle, one person sits on the immediate left and one on the immediate right, so there are 2 immediate neighbours.

Shortcut: Circular arrangements wrap around; first and last are neighbours.

Q28. In analogy 'Tree : Forest', identify the relationship.

Dictionary ordermissing numberEasyTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. Number pattern
  2. B. Synonym
  3. C. Part-whole
  4. D. Opposite meaning
Show answer and explanation

Answer: C. Part-whole

Explanation: The pair Tree:Forest follows the relationship 'Part-whole'. This type of relationship helps solve analogy questions quickly.

Shortcut: Name the relation first; do not jump to options.

Show answer and explanation

Answer: B. 37

Explanation: Terms add square numbers: 1², 2², 3², 4², 5². Next uses 6², so answer is 1+36 = 37.

Shortcut: After simple difference, check square/cube patterns.

Q57. What is the value of 7 % 5 in C-style programming?

Coding-decodingdry runMediumTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. 12
  2. B. 1
  3. C. 2
  4. D. 35
Show answer and explanation

Answer: C. 2

Explanation: The % operator gives remainder. 7 divided by 5 leaves remainder 2.

Shortcut: Modulo gives remainder, not quotient.

Q58. Given array [2, 4, 9, 5], what value will a maximum-finding loop return?

Letter codingloop traceMediumTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. 4
  2. B. 20
  3. C. 2
  4. D. 9
Show answer and explanation

Answer: D. 9

Explanation: Compare each element and keep the largest. The maximum among [2, 4, 9, 5] is 9.

Shortcut: Initialize max with first element, then compare one by one.

Q59. Classify the string 'tcs' after comparing it with its reverse.

Number codingarray/string logicHardTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. Not palindrome
  2. B. Anagram
  3. C. Empty string
  4. D. Sorted string
Show answer and explanation

Answer: A. Not palindrome

Explanation: Reverse of 'tcs' is 'sct'. Since it does not match, it is not palindrome.

Shortcut: Palindrome check: compare first with last, second with second last.

Q60. What does a factorial function return for input 5?

Symbol codingoutput predictionEasyConcept Practice
  1. A. 119
  2. B. 24
  3. C. 125
  4. D. 120
Show answer and explanation

Answer: D. 120

Explanation: Factorial means product from 1 to 5. 5! = 120.

Shortcut: Recursive factorial: fact(n)=n×fact(n-1), base fact(0)=1.

Q61. Find the next number: 3, 6, 11, 18, 27, ?

Meaningful word formationalternate patternMediumTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. 32
  2. B. 45
  3. C. 33
  4. D. 38
Show answer and explanation

Answer: D. 38

Explanation: Terms add square numbers: 1², 2², 3², 4², 5². Next uses 6², so answer is 2+36 = 38.

Shortcut: After simple difference, check square/cube patterns.

Q62. In a family tree, P is Q's father and Q is R's mother. How is Q related to R?

Word arrangementblood relationMediumTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. Mother
  2. B. Aunt
  3. C. Daughter
  4. D. Sister
Show answer and explanation

Answer: A. Mother

Explanation: The statement directly says Q is R's mother.

Shortcut: Do not overcomplicate direct relation statements.

Q63. In analogy 'Bird : Nest', identify the relationship.

Dictionary ordermissing numberHardTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. Animal-home
  2. B. Synonym
  3. C. Number pattern
  4. D. Opposite meaning
Show answer and explanation

Answer: A. Animal-home

Explanation: The pair Bird:Nest follows the relationship 'Animal-home'. This type of relationship helps solve analogy questions quickly.

Shortcut: Name the relation first; do not jump to options.

Q64. What is the value of 9 % 5 in C-style programming?

Coding-decodingarray/string logicEasyTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. 14
  2. B. 1
  3. C. 4
  4. D. 45
Show answer and explanation

Answer: C. 4

Explanation: The % operator gives remainder. 9 divided by 5 leaves remainder 4.

Shortcut: Modulo gives remainder, not quotient.

Q65. Given array [2, 4, 9, 4], what value will a maximum-finding loop return?

Letter codingoutput predictionMediumConcept Practice
  1. A. 2
  2. B. 19
  3. C. 4
  4. D. 9
Show answer and explanation

Answer: D. 9

Explanation: Compare each element and keep the largest. The maximum among [2, 4, 9, 4] is 9.

Shortcut: Initialize max with first element, then compare one by one.

Select answers and submit to update LocalStorage score, accuracy, topic-wise score, and weak areas.

Quiz 2. Given array [2, 4, 9, 5], what value will a maximum-finding loop return?

Quiz 7. If each letter is shifted forward by 3, what will C become?

End-of-Day Revision Card — Day 11

10-point summary

  1. Revise the definition and purpose of Coding-decoding.
  2. Write formulas once by hand before attempting speed questions.
  3. Solve easy questions first to build accuracy.
  4. Use option elimination wherever full calculation is long.
  5. Track every mistake in the weak-area dashboard.
  6. Reattempt wrong quiz questions without seeing the answer.
  7. Mark completed topics only after solving examples.
  8. Keep verbal practice short but consistent.
  9. Use shortcuts only after understanding the concept.
  10. End the day by reviewing tomorrow’s topic list.

Formula recap

  • Loop dry run: Track variable after each iteration — Use a small table.
  • Modulo: a % b = remainder — Useful for digit and divisibility code.
  • Array traversal: Index 0 to n-1 — Avoid out-of-bound errors.
  • Arithmetic series: Check difference — Most common first check.
  • Double difference: Check change in differences — For quadratic patterns.

Tricks recap

  • Coding-decoding: Before solving Coding-decoding, identify the exact concept from the wording; the first correct identification saves most time.
  • Letter coding: Before solving Letter coding, identify the exact concept from the wording; the first correct identification saves most time.
  • Number coding: Before solving Number coding, identify the exact concept from the wording; the first correct identification saves most time.
  • Symbol coding: Before solving Symbol coding, identify the exact concept from the wording; the first correct identification saves most time.

Mistake checklist

  • Wrong base value
  • Wrong unit conversion
  • Misread option
  • Skipped condition
  • No revision of incorrect answers

Weak-area revision advice: Any topic below 60% must be revised before moving to hard questions.

Tomorrow’s preparation preview: Day 12 — Permutations, Combinations, Probability Basics.

Day 12 • 7 June 2026

Day 12 — Permutations, Combinations, Probability Basics

Study objective: Build practical command over Permutations and combinations, Fundamental counting principle, Arrangements, Selection and apply it in TCS NQT-style timed questions.

Previous DayNext Day
Day mini TOC: Permutations and combinations Fundamental counting principle Arrangements Selection Repetition and non-repetition Basic probability introduction PracticeQuizScoreRevision

Topics covered

  • Permutations and combinations
  • Fundamental counting principle
  • Arrangements
  • Selection
  • Repetition and non-repetition
  • Basic probability introduction
Permutations and combinations

Permutations and combinations measures chance. Count favourable outcomes and total possible outcomes carefully. For counting-based probability, decide whether order matters before using permutation or combination.

  1. Step 1: Read the question and identify that it belongs to Permutations and combinations.
  2. Step 2: Write the given values or conditions in short form.
  3. Step 3: Choose the correct rule, formula, or logic pattern.
  4. Step 4: Solve neatly and compare with options.
  5. Step 5: Recheck units, signs, and hidden conditions before final answer.

Tricks to Remember

Concept trick

Before solving Permutations and combinations, identify the exact concept from the wording; the first correct identification saves most time.

Formula shortcut

Write only the necessary formula for Permutations and combinations; avoid using long methods when an option-based shortcut works.

Memory method

Remember Favourable/total trick as the first shortcut for this topic.

Pattern recognition trick

Look for repeated structures, ratios, signs, keywords, and option patterns in Permutations and combinations.

Option elimination trick

Eliminate impossible options using parity, magnitude, grammar sense, or boundary values.

Common trap

The usual trap in Permutations and combinations is using the wrong base, wrong unit, wrong order, or assuming extra information.

30-second solving method

Convert the question into one line of formula or one small diagram, then test the closest option.

Fast solving method for TCS NQT

Use Favourable/total trick, Order matters trick and avoid lengthy manual expansion.

Solved Examples

Example 1: Probability of getting even number on a die.
Even outcomes = 2,4,6 = 3. Total = 6. Probability = 3/6 = 1/2.
Shortcut: Favourable/total.
Example 2: AP: a=5, d=3, find 6th term.
T6 = 5+(6-1)×3 = 20.
Shortcut: Use nth term formula.

Common Mistakes

  • Starting Permutations and combinations without identifying the exact concept.
  • Copying numbers incorrectly from the question.
  • Selecting the first matching option without checking all conditions.
  • Ignoring units, signs, order, tense, or base value.
  • Not reviewing wrong answers after practice.
Fundamental counting principle

Fundamental counting principle is the foundation of quantitative aptitude. Start by understanding what a number represents, how digits contribute through place value, and how divisibility or remainder rules reduce calculation. In TCS NQT, these questions are usually short but accuracy matters. Solve by checking the structure of the number before doing long calculation.

  1. Step 1: Read the question and identify that it belongs to Fundamental counting principle.
  2. Step 2: Write the given values or conditions in short form.
  3. Step 3: Choose the correct rule, formula, or logic pattern.
  4. Step 4: Solve neatly and compare with options.
  5. Step 5: Recheck units, signs, and hidden conditions before final answer.

Tricks to Remember

Concept trick

Before solving Fundamental counting principle, identify the exact concept from the wording; the first correct identification saves most time.

Formula shortcut

Write only the necessary formula for Fundamental counting principle; avoid using long methods when an option-based shortcut works.

Memory method

Remember Last digit trick as the first shortcut for this topic.

Pattern recognition trick

Look for repeated structures, ratios, signs, keywords, and option patterns in Fundamental counting principle.

Option elimination trick

Eliminate impossible options using parity, magnitude, grammar sense, or boundary values.

Common trap

The usual trap in Fundamental counting principle is using the wrong base, wrong unit, wrong order, or assuming extra information.

30-second solving method

Convert the question into one line of formula or one small diagram, then test the closest option.

Fast solving method for TCS NQT

Use Last digit trick, Unit digit trick and avoid lengthy manual expansion.

Solved Examples

Example 1: Find whether 729 is divisible by 9.
Digit sum = 7+2+9 = 18. Since 18 is divisible by 9, 729 is divisible by 9.
Shortcut: Digit sum rule avoids division.
Example 2: Find LCM of 12 and 18.
HCF is 6. LCM = 12×18/6 = 36.
Shortcut: Use product/HCF.

Common Mistakes

  • Starting Fundamental counting principle without identifying the exact concept.
  • Copying numbers incorrectly from the question.
  • Selecting the first matching option without checking all conditions.
  • Ignoring units, signs, order, tense, or base value.
  • Not reviewing wrong answers after practice.
Arrangements

Arrangements needs structured placement. Draw slots for linear arrangement, circles for circular arrangement, and family-tree arrows for blood relations. Place definite information first, then handle conditions.

  1. Step 1: Read the question and identify that it belongs to Arrangements.
  2. Step 2: Write the given values or conditions in short form.
  3. Step 3: Choose the correct rule, formula, or logic pattern.
  4. Step 4: Solve neatly and compare with options.
  5. Step 5: Recheck units, signs, and hidden conditions before final answer.

Tricks to Remember

Concept trick

Before solving Arrangements, identify the exact concept from the wording; the first correct identification saves most time.

Formula shortcut

Write only the necessary formula for Arrangements; avoid using long methods when an option-based shortcut works.

Memory method

Remember Fixed-position trick as the first shortcut for this topic.

Pattern recognition trick

Look for repeated structures, ratios, signs, keywords, and option patterns in Arrangements.

Option elimination trick

Eliminate impossible options using parity, magnitude, grammar sense, or boundary values.

Common trap

The usual trap in Arrangements is using the wrong base, wrong unit, wrong order, or assuming extra information.

30-second solving method

Convert the question into one line of formula or one small diagram, then test the closest option.

Fast solving method for TCS NQT

Use Fixed-position trick, Family tree arrow trick and avoid lengthy manual expansion.

Solved Examples

Example 1: Find next: 3, 7, 11, 15, ?
Difference is +4, so next = 19.
Shortcut: Check difference first.
Example 2: A>B and B>C. Who is tallest?
A is tallest because A>B>C.
Shortcut: Write comparison chain.

Common Mistakes

  • Starting Arrangements without identifying the exact concept.
  • Copying numbers incorrectly from the question.
  • Selecting the first matching option without checking all conditions.
  • Ignoring units, signs, order, tense, or base value.
  • Not reviewing wrong answers after practice.
Selection

Selection is the foundation of quantitative aptitude. Start by understanding what a number represents, how digits contribute through place value, and how divisibility or remainder rules reduce calculation. In TCS NQT, these questions are usually short but accuracy matters. Solve by checking the structure of the number before doing long calculation.

  1. Step 1: Read the question and identify that it belongs to Selection.
  2. Step 2: Write the given values or conditions in short form.
  3. Step 3: Choose the correct rule, formula, or logic pattern.
  4. Step 4: Solve neatly and compare with options.
  5. Step 5: Recheck units, signs, and hidden conditions before final answer.

Tricks to Remember

Concept trick

Before solving Selection, identify the exact concept from the wording; the first correct identification saves most time.

Formula shortcut

Write only the necessary formula for Selection; avoid using long methods when an option-based shortcut works.

Memory method

Remember Last digit trick as the first shortcut for this topic.

Pattern recognition trick

Look for repeated structures, ratios, signs, keywords, and option patterns in Selection.

Option elimination trick

Eliminate impossible options using parity, magnitude, grammar sense, or boundary values.

Common trap

The usual trap in Selection is using the wrong base, wrong unit, wrong order, or assuming extra information.

30-second solving method

Convert the question into one line of formula or one small diagram, then test the closest option.

Fast solving method for TCS NQT

Use Last digit trick, Unit digit trick and avoid lengthy manual expansion.

Solved Examples

Example 1: Find whether 729 is divisible by 9.
Digit sum = 7+2+9 = 18. Since 18 is divisible by 9, 729 is divisible by 9.
Shortcut: Digit sum rule avoids division.
Example 2: Find LCM of 12 and 18.
HCF is 6. LCM = 12×18/6 = 36.
Shortcut: Use product/HCF.

Common Mistakes

  • Starting Selection without identifying the exact concept.
  • Copying numbers incorrectly from the question.
  • Selecting the first matching option without checking all conditions.
  • Ignoring units, signs, order, tense, or base value.
  • Not reviewing wrong answers after practice.
Repetition and non-repetition

Repetition and non-repetition is the foundation of quantitative aptitude. Start by understanding what a number represents, how digits contribute through place value, and how divisibility or remainder rules reduce calculation. In TCS NQT, these questions are usually short but accuracy matters. Solve by checking the structure of the number before doing long calculation.

  1. Step 1: Read the question and identify that it belongs to Repetition and non-repetition.
  2. Step 2: Write the given values or conditions in short form.
  3. Step 3: Choose the correct rule, formula, or logic pattern.
  4. Step 4: Solve neatly and compare with options.
  5. Step 5: Recheck units, signs, and hidden conditions before final answer.

Fast solving method for TCS NQT

Solved Examples

Example 1: Find whether 729 is divisible by 9.
Digit sum = 7+2+9 = 18. Since 18 is divisible by 9, 729 is divisible by 9.
Shortcut: Digit sum rule avoids division.
Example 2: Find LCM of 12 and 18.
HCF is 6. LCM = 12×18/6 = 36.
Shortcut: Use product/HCF.

Common Mistakes

Basic probability introduction

Basic probability introduction measures chance. Count favourable outcomes and total possible outcomes carefully. For counting-based probability, decide whether order matters before using permutation or combination.

  1. Step 1: Read the question and identify that it belongs to Basic probability introduction.
  2. Step 2: Write the given values or conditions in short form.
  3. Step 3: Choose the correct rule, formula, or logic pattern.
  4. Step 4: Solve neatly and compare with options.
  5. Step 5: Recheck units, signs, and hidden conditions before final answer.

Tricks to Remember

Concept trick

Before solving Basic probability introduction, identify the exact concept from the wording; the first correct identification saves most time.

Formula shortcut

Write only the necessary formula for Basic probability introduction; avoid using long methods when an option-based shortcut works.

Memory method

Remember Favourable/total trick as the first shortcut for this topic.

Pattern recognition trick

Look for repeated structures, ratios, signs, keywords, and option patterns in Basic probability introduction.

Option elimination trick

Eliminate impossible options using parity, magnitude, grammar sense, or boundary values.

Common trap

The usual trap in Basic probability introduction is using the wrong base, wrong unit, wrong order, or assuming extra information.

30-second solving method

Convert the question into one line of formula or one small diagram, then test the closest option.

Fast solving method for TCS NQT

Use Favourable/total trick, Order matters trick and avoid lengthy manual expansion.

Solved Examples

Example 1: Probability of getting even number on a die.
Even outcomes = 2,4,6 = 3. Total = 6. Probability = 3/6 = 1/2.
Shortcut: Favourable/total.
Example 2: AP: a=5, d=3, find 6th term.
T6 = 5+(6-1)×3 = 20.
Shortcut: Use nth term formula.

Common Mistakes

  • Starting Basic probability introduction without identifying the exact concept.
  • Copying numbers incorrectly from the question.
  • Selecting the first matching option without checking all conditions.
  • Ignoring units, signs, order, tense, or base value.
  • Not reviewing wrong answers after practice.

Important Formulas and Rules

Formula/RuleExpressionWhen to use
ProbabilityFavourable / TotalCore formula.
CombinationnCrSelection, order does not matter.
PermutationnPrArrangement, order matters.
Divisibility by 2Last digit is evenUse for quick even/odd and factor checks.
Divisibility by 3Sum of digits divisible by 3Use before doing long division.
Divisibility by 9Sum of digits divisible by 9Useful for remainders and shortcuts.
Linear seatingUse slotsMark facing direction.
Circular seatingFix one personReduces rotation confusion.
Blood relationDecode inside outwardConvert each phrase slowly.

Practice Question Bank — 65 Questions

All questions below are original TCS NQT PYQ-style or concept-practice questions with answers and explanations.

Q1. A coin is tossed once. What is the probability of getting heads?

Permutations and combinationsdiceMediumTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. 2/3
  2. B. 1/4
  3. C. 1/2
  4. D. 1/3
Show answer and explanation

Answer: C. 1/2

Explanation: A fair coin has two equally likely outcomes: H and T. Favourable outcomes for heads = 1. Probability = 1/2.

Shortcut: For one fair coin, heads and tails are equally likely.

Q2. Find the remainder when (6 × 31 + 12) is divided by 7.

Fundamental counting principleremainderMediumTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. 5
  2. B. 2
  3. C. 4
  4. D. 3
Show answer and explanation

Answer: B. 2

Explanation: First calculate 6 × 31 + 12 = 198. Divide by 7. The remainder is 2.

Shortcut: For remainder questions, reduce each term by the divisor before multiplying.

Q3. In a circular table, each person has exactly how many immediate neighbours?

Arrangementsfamily treeHardTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. 3
  2. B. 2
  3. C. 1
  4. D. 4
Show answer and explanation

Answer: B. 2

Explanation: In a circle, one person sits on the immediate left and one on the immediate right, so there are 2 immediate neighbours.

Shortcut: Circular arrangements wrap around; first and last are neighbours.

Q4. Classify the number 45.

Selectionplace valueEasyTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. Even
  2. B. Odd only
  3. C. Co-prime only
  4. D. Composite
Show answer and explanation

Answer: D. Composite

Explanation: Check divisibility up to √45. Since a divisor is found, 45 is composite.

Shortcut: To test prime, check only divisors up to square root.

Q5. In the number 7809, what is the place value of the digit 8?

Repetition and non-repetitiondivisibilityMediumConcept Practice
  1. A. 8000
  2. B. 8
  3. C. 800
  4. D. 80
Show answer and explanation

Answer: C. 800

Explanation: Place value means digit multiplied by its position value. In 7809, digit 8 is at the 100 place, so place value = 8 × 100 = 800.

Shortcut: Read from right: ones, tens, hundreds, thousands. Multiply the digit by its place.

Q6. A coin is tossed once. What is the probability of getting heads?

Basic probability introductiondiceMediumTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. 2/3
  2. B. 1/4
  3. C. 1/2
  4. D. 1/3
Show answer and explanation

Answer: C. 1/2

Explanation: A fair coin has two equally likely outcomes: H and T. Favourable outcomes for heads = 1. Probability = 1/2.

Shortcut: For one fair coin, heads and tails are equally likely.

Q7. How many ways can 2 students be selected from 7 students?

Permutations and combinationscardsHardTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. 21
  2. B. 14
  3. C. 23
  4. D. 19
Show answer and explanation

Answer: A. 21

Explanation: Selection uses combination: nCr = 7C2 = 21.

Shortcut: Use combination when order does not matter.

Q8. Using divisibility rules, which statement is true for 122?

Fundamental counting principleprime/compositeEasyTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. divisible by 9
  2. B. not divisible by 3
  3. C. multiple of 11
  4. D. prime number
Show answer and explanation

Answer: B. not divisible by 3

Explanation: Sum of digits of 122 is 5. A number is divisible by 3 when the digit sum is divisible by 3. Therefore, 122 is not divisible by 3.

Shortcut: For 3 and 9, check digit sum instead of division.

Q9. Pointing to a boy, Neha says, 'He is the son of my father's only son.' How is the boy related to Neha?

ArrangementspuzzleMediumTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. Brother
  2. B. Uncle
  3. C. Nephew
  4. D. Father
Show answer and explanation

Answer: C. Nephew

Explanation: Father's only son is Neha's brother if Neha is female, but the son of Neha's brother is nephew. However the question says pointing to a boy and asks relation to Neha; if Neha's father's only son is Neha's brother, his son is Neha's nephew.

Shortcut: Convert each phrase from inside outward.

Q10. In the number 7854, what is the place value of the digit 8?

SelectiondivisibilityMediumConcept Practice
  1. A. 8000
  2. B. 8
  3. C. 800
  4. D. 80
Show answer and explanation

Answer: C. 800

Explanation: Place value means digit multiplied by its position value. In 7854, digit 8 is at the 100 place, so place value = 8 × 100 = 800.

Shortcut: Read from right: ones, tens, hundreds, thousands. Multiply the digit by its place.

Q11. What is the unit digit of 10^46?

Repetition and non-repetitionunit digitHardTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. 6
  2. B. 0
  3. C. 2
  4. D. 4
Show answer and explanation

Answer: B. 0

Explanation: Unit digits follow cycles. For base 10, calculate powers modulo 10. Since 46 is reduced within the unit-digit cycle, the unit digit is 0.

Shortcut: Use the unit digit cycle; never expand the full power.

Q12. How many ways can 2 students be selected from 7 students?

Basic probability introductioncardsEasyTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. 21
  2. B. 14
  3. C. 23
  4. D. 19
Show answer and explanation

Answer: A. 21

Explanation: Selection uses combination: nCr = 7C2 = 21.

Shortcut: Use combination when order does not matter.

Q13. How many arrangements of 2 students can be made from 5 students?

Permutations and combinationseventsMediumTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. 18
  2. B. 10
  3. C. 20
  4. D. 22
Show answer and explanation

Answer: C. 20

Explanation: Arrangement uses permutation: nPr = 5P2 = 20.

Shortcut: Use permutation when order matters.

Q14. Classify the number 55.

Fundamental counting principleplace valueMediumTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. Even
  2. B. Odd only
  3. C. Co-prime only
  4. D. Composite
Show answer and explanation

Answer: D. Composite

Explanation: Check divisibility up to √55. Since a divisor is found, 55 is composite.

Shortcut: To test prime, check only divisors up to square root.

Q15. In a circular table, each person has exactly how many immediate neighbours?

ArrangementslinearHardConcept Practice
  1. A. 3
  2. B. 2
  3. C. 1
  4. D. 4
Show answer and explanation

Answer: B. 2

Explanation: In a circle, one person sits on the immediate left and one on the immediate right, so there are 2 immediate neighbours.

Shortcut: Circular arrangements wrap around; first and last are neighbours.

Q16. What is the unit digit of 7^51?

Selectionunit digitEasyTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. 7
  2. B. 5
  3. C. 3
  4. D. 9
Show answer and explanation

Answer: C. 3

Explanation: Unit digits follow cycles. For base 7, calculate powers modulo 10. Since 51 is reduced within the unit-digit cycle, the unit digit is 3.

Shortcut: Use the unit digit cycle; never expand the full power.

Q17. Find the remainder when (12 × 46 + 12) is divided by 8.

Repetition and non-repetitionremainderMediumTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. 4
  2. B. 5
  3. C. 7
  4. D. 6
Show answer and explanation

Answer: A. 4

Explanation: First calculate 12 × 46 + 12 = 564. Divide by 8. The remainder is 4.

Shortcut: For remainder questions, reduce each term by the divisor before multiplying.

Q18. How many arrangements of 2 students can be made from 6 students?

Basic probability introductioneventsMediumTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. 32
  2. B. 30
  3. C. 15
  4. D. 28
Show answer and explanation

Answer: B. 30

Explanation: Arrangement uses permutation: nPr = 6P2 = 30.

Shortcut: Use permutation when order matters.

Q19. One card is drawn from a standard deck of 52 cards. What is the probability of drawing a heart?

Permutations and combinationscountingHardTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. 1/52
  2. B. 4/52
  3. C. 13/52
  4. D. 26/52
Show answer and explanation

Answer: C. 13/52

Explanation: There are 13 hearts in 52 cards. Probability = 13/52 = 1/4.

Shortcut: In a deck: 4 suits, 13 cards each.

Q20. In the number 7944, what is the place value of the digit 9?

Fundamental counting principledivisibilityEasyConcept Practice
  1. A. 900
  2. B. 90
  3. C. 9000
  4. D. 9
Show answer and explanation

Answer: A. 900

Explanation: Place value means digit multiplied by its position value. In 7944, digit 9 is at the 100 place, so place value = 9 × 100 = 900.

Shortcut: Read from right: ones, tens, hundreds, thousands. Multiply the digit by its place.

Q21. Pointing to a boy, Neha says, 'He is the son of my father's only son.' How is the boy related to Neha?

ArrangementscircularMediumTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. Brother
  2. B. Uncle
  3. C. Nephew
  4. D. Father
Show answer and explanation

Answer: C. Nephew

Explanation: Father's only son is Neha's brother if Neha is female, but the son of Neha's brother is nephew. However the question says pointing to a boy and asks relation to Neha; if Neha's father's only son is Neha's brother, his son is Neha's nephew.

Shortcut: Convert each phrase from inside outward.

Q22. Find the remainder when (8 × 51 + 12) is divided by 6.

SelectionremainderMediumTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. 0
  2. B. 2
  3. C. 1
  4. D. 3
Show answer and explanation

Answer: A. 0

Explanation: First calculate 8 × 51 + 12 = 420. Divide by 6. The remainder is 0.

Shortcut: For remainder questions, reduce each term by the divisor before multiplying.

Q23. Using divisibility rules, which statement is true for 137?

Repetition and non-repetitionprime/compositeHardTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. divisible by 9
  2. B. not divisible by 3
  3. C. multiple of 11
  4. D. prime number
Show answer and explanation

Answer: B. not divisible by 3

Explanation: Sum of digits of 137 is 11. A number is divisible by 3 when the digit sum is divisible by 3. Therefore, 137 is not divisible by 3.

Shortcut: For 3 and 9, check digit sum instead of division.

Q24. One card is drawn from a standard deck of 52 cards. What is the probability of drawing a heart?

Basic probability introductioncountingEasyTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. 1/52
  2. B. 4/52
  3. C. 13/52
  4. D. 26/52
Show answer and explanation

Answer: C. 13/52

Explanation: There are 13 hearts in 52 cards. Probability = 13/52 = 1/4.

Shortcut: In a deck: 4 suits, 13 cards each.

Q25. A fair die is rolled once. What is the probability of getting 4?

Permutations and combinationscoinsMediumConcept Practice
  1. A. 1/6
  2. B. 1/3
  3. C. 5/6
  4. D. 1/2
Show answer and explanation

Answer: A. 1/6

Explanation: There is 1 favourable outcome {4} out of 6 total outcomes. Probability = 1/6.

Shortcut: Probability = favourable outcomes / total outcomes.

Q26. What is the unit digit of 9^61?

Fundamental counting principleunit digitMediumTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. 5
  2. B. 3
  3. C. 1
  4. D. 9
Show answer and explanation

Answer: D. 9

Explanation: Unit digits follow cycles. For base 9, calculate powers modulo 10. Since 61 is reduced within the unit-digit cycle, the unit digit is 9.

Shortcut: Use the unit digit cycle; never expand the full power.

Q27. In a circular table, each person has exactly how many immediate neighbours?

Arrangementsblood relationHardTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. 3
  2. B. 2
  3. C. 1
  4. D. 4
Show answer and explanation

Answer: B. 2

Explanation: In a circle, one person sits on the immediate left and one on the immediate right, so there are 2 immediate neighbours.

Shortcut: Circular arrangements wrap around; first and last are neighbours.

Q28. Using divisibility rules, which statement is true for 142?

Selectionprime/compositeEasyTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. divisible by 9
  2. B. not divisible by 3
  3. C. multiple of 11
  4. D. prime number
Show answer and explanation

Answer: B. not divisible by 3

Explanation: Sum of digits of 142 is 7. A number is divisible by 3 when the digit sum is divisible by 3. Therefore, 142 is not divisible by 3.

Shortcut: For 3 and 9, check digit sum instead of division.

Q29. Classify the number 70.

Repetition and non-repetitionplace valueMediumTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. Even
  2. B. Odd only
  3. C. Co-prime only
  4. D. Composite
Show answer and explanation

Answer: D. Composite

Explanation: Check divisibility up to √70. Since a divisor is found, 70 is composite.

Shortcut: To test prime, check only divisors up to square root.

Q30. A fair die is rolled once. What is the probability of getting 4?

Basic probability introductioncoinsMediumConcept Practice
  1. A. 1/6
  2. B. 1/3
  3. C. 5/6
  4. D. 1/2
Show answer and explanation

Answer: A. 1/6

Explanation: There is 1 favourable outcome {4} out of 6 total outcomes. Probability = 1/6.

Shortcut: Probability = favourable outcomes / total outcomes.

Q31. A coin is tossed once. What is the probability of getting heads?

Permutations and combinationsdiceHardTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. 2/3
  2. B. 1/4
  3. C. 1/2
  4. D. 1/3
Show answer and explanation

Answer: C. 1/2

Explanation: A fair coin has two equally likely outcomes: H and T. Favourable outcomes for heads = 1. Probability = 1/2.

Shortcut: For one fair coin, heads and tails are equally likely.

Q32. Find the remainder when (9 × 61 + 12) is divided by 9.

Fundamental counting principleremainderEasyTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. 6
  2. B. 4
  3. C. 5
  4. D. 3
Show answer and explanation

Answer: D. 3

Explanation: First calculate 9 × 61 + 12 = 561. Divide by 9. The remainder is 3.

Shortcut: For remainder questions, reduce each term by the divisor before multiplying.

Arrangementsfamily treeMediumTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. Brother
  2. B. Uncle
  3. C. Nephew
  4. D. Father
Show answer and explanation

Answer: C. Nephew

Explanation: Father's only son is Neha's brother if Neha is female, but the son of Neha's brother is nephew. However the question says pointing to a boy and asks relation to Neha; if Neha's father's only son is Neha's brother, his son is Neha's nephew.

Shortcut: Convert each phrase from inside outward.

Q34. Classify the number 75.

Selectionplace valueMediumTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. Even
  2. B. Odd only
  3. C. Co-prime only
  4. D. Composite
Show answer and explanation

Answer: D. Composite

Explanation: Check divisibility up to √75. Since a divisor is found, 75 is composite.

Shortcut: To test prime, check only divisors up to square root.

Q35. In the number 8079, what is the place value of the digit 0?

Repetition and non-repetitiondivisibilityHardConcept Practice
  1. A. 16
  2. B. 5
  3. C. 8
  4. D. 0
Show answer and explanation

Answer: D. 0

Explanation: Place value means digit multiplied by its position value. In 8079, digit 0 is at the 100 place, so place value = 0 × 100 = 0.

Shortcut: Read from right: ones, tens, hundreds, thousands. Multiply the digit by its place.

Q36. A coin is tossed once. What is the probability of getting heads?

Basic probability introductiondiceEasyTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. 2/3
  2. B. 1/4
  3. C. 1/2
  4. D. 1/3
Show answer and explanation

Answer: C. 1/2

Explanation: A fair coin has two equally likely outcomes: H and T. Favourable outcomes for heads = 1. Probability = 1/2.

Shortcut: For one fair coin, heads and tails are equally likely.

Q37. How many ways can 2 students be selected from 7 students?

Permutations and combinationscardsMediumTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. 21
  2. B. 14
  3. C. 23
  4. D. 19
Show answer and explanation

Answer: A. 21

Explanation: Selection uses combination: nCr = 7C2 = 21.

Shortcut: Use combination when order does not matter.

Q38. Using divisibility rules, which statement is true for 152?

Fundamental counting principleprime/compositeMediumTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. divisible by 9
  2. B. not divisible by 3
  3. C. multiple of 11
  4. D. prime number
Show answer and explanation

Answer: B. not divisible by 3

Explanation: Sum of digits of 152 is 8. A number is divisible by 3 when the digit sum is divisible by 3. Therefore, 152 is not divisible by 3.

Shortcut: For 3 and 9, check digit sum instead of division.

Q39. In a circular table, each person has exactly how many immediate neighbours?

ArrangementspuzzleHardTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. 3
  2. B. 2
  3. C. 1
  4. D. 4
Show answer and explanation

Answer: B. 2

Explanation: In a circle, one person sits on the immediate left and one on the immediate right, so there are 2 immediate neighbours.

Shortcut: Circular arrangements wrap around; first and last are neighbours.

SelectiondivisibilityEasyConcept Practice
  1. A. 1
  2. B. 100
  3. C. 1000
  4. D. 10
Show answer and explanation

Answer: B. 100

Explanation: Place value means digit multiplied by its position value. In 8124, digit 1 is at the 100 place, so place value = 1 × 100 = 100.

Shortcut: Read from right: ones, tens, hundreds, thousands. Multiply the digit by its place.

Q41. What is the unit digit of 8^76?

Repetition and non-repetitionunit digitMediumTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. 2
  2. B. 0
  3. C. 8
  4. D. 6
Show answer and explanation

Answer: D. 6

Explanation: Unit digits follow cycles. For base 8, calculate powers modulo 10. Since 76 is reduced within the unit-digit cycle, the unit digit is 6.

Shortcut: Use the unit digit cycle; never expand the full power.

Q42. How many ways can 2 students be selected from 7 students?

Basic probability introductioncardsMediumTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. 21
  2. B. 14
  3. C. 23
  4. D. 19
Show answer and explanation

Answer: A. 21

Explanation: Selection uses combination: nCr = 7C2 = 21.

Shortcut: Use combination when order does not matter.

Permutations and combinationseventsHardTCS NQT PYQ-style
  1. A. 44
  2. B. 21
  3. C. 42
  4. D. 40
Show answer and explanation

Answer: C. 42

Explanation: Arrangement uses permutation: nPr = 7P2 = 42.

Shortcut: Use permutation when order matters.

End-of-Day Revision Card — Day 12

10-point summary

  1. Revise the definition and purpose of Permutations and combinations.
  2. Write formulas once by hand before attempting speed questions.
  3. Solve easy questions first to build accuracy.
  4. Use option elimination wherever full calculation is long.
  5. Track every mistake in the weak-area dashboard.
  6. Reattempt wrong quiz questions without seeing the answer.
  7. Mark completed topics only after solving examples.
  8. Keep verbal practice short but consistent.
  9. Use shortcuts only after understanding the concept.
  10. End the day by reviewing tomorrow’s topic list.

Formula recap

  • Probability: Favourable / Total — Core formula.
  • Combination: nCr — Selection, order does not matter.
  • Permutation: nPr — Arrangement, order matters.
  • Divisibility by 2: Last digit is even — Use for quick even/odd and factor checks.
  • Divisibility by 3: Sum of digits divisible by 3 — Use before doing long division.

Tricks recap

  • Permutations and combinations: Before solving Permutations and combinations, identify the exact concept from the wording; the first correct identification saves most time.
  • Fundamental counting principle: Before solving Fundamental counting principle, identify the exact concept from the wording; the first correct identification saves most time.
  • Arrangements: Before solving Arrangements, identify the exact concept from the wording; the first correct identification saves most time.
  • Selection: Before solving Selection, identify the exact concept from the wording; the first correct identification saves most time.

Mistake checklist

  • Wrong base value
  • Wrong unit conversion
  • Misread option
  • Skipped condition
  • No revision of incorrect answers

Weak-area revision advice: Any topic below 60% must be revised before moving to hard questions.

Tomorrow’s preparation preview: Day 13 — Probability + Foundation Mock 1.

Day 13 • 8 June 2026

Day 13 — Probability + Foundation Mock 1

Study objective: Build practical command over Probability, Events, Simple probability, Dice and apply it in TCS NQT-style timed questions.

Previous DayNext Day
Day mini TOC: Probability Events Simple probability Dice Coins Cards Probability shortcuts Foundation mock test 1 PracticeQuizScoreRevision

Topics covered

  • Probability
  • Events
  • Simple probability
  • Dice
  • Coins
  • Cards
  • Probability shortcuts
  • Foundation mock test 1
Probability

Probability measures chance. Count favourable outcomes and total possible outcomes carefully. For counting-based probability, decide whether order matters before using permutation or combination.

  1. Step 1: Read the question and identify that it belongs to Probability.
  2. Step 2: Write the given values or conditions in short form.
  3. Step 3: Choose the correct rule, formula, or logic pattern.
  4. Step 4: Solve neatly and compare with options.
  5. Step 5: Recheck units, signs, and hidden conditions before final answer.

Tricks to Remember

Concept trick

Before solving Probability, identify the exact concept from the wording; the first correct identification saves most time.

Formula shortcut

Write only the necessary formula for Probability; avoid using long methods when an option-based shortcut works.

Memory method

Remember Favourable/total trick as the first shortcut for this topic.

Pattern recognition trick

Look for repeated structures, ratios, signs, keywords, and option patterns in Probability.

Option elimination trick

Eliminate impossible options using parity, magnitude, grammar sense, or boundary values.

Common trap

The usual trap in Probability is using the wrong base, wrong unit, wrong order, or assuming extra information.

30-second solving method

Convert the question into one line of formula or one small diagram, then test the closest option.

Fast solving method for TCS NQT

Use Favourable/total trick, Order matters trick and avoid lengthy manual expansion.

Solved Examples

Example 1: Probability of getting even number on a die.
Even outcomes = 2,4,6 = 3. Total = 6. Probability = 3/6 = 1/2.
Shortcut: Favourable/total.
Example 2: AP: a=5, d=3, find 6th term.
T6 = 5+(6-1)×3 = 20.
Shortcut: Use nth term formula.

Common Mistakes

  • Starting Probability without identifying the exact concept.
  • Copying numbers incorrectly from the question.
  • Selecting the first matching option without checking all conditions.
  • Ignoring units, signs, order, tense, or base value.
  • Not reviewing wrong answers after practice.
Events

Events measures chance. Count favourable outcomes and total possible outcomes carefully. For counting-based probability, decide whether order matters before using permutation or combination.

  1. Step 1: Read the question and identify that it belongs to Events.
  2. Step 2: Write the given values or conditions in short form.
  3. Step 3: Choose the correct rule, formula, or logic pattern.
  4. Step 4: Solve neatly and compare with options.
  5. Step 5: Recheck units, signs, and hidden conditions before final answer.

Tricks to Remember

Concept trick

Before solving Events, identify the exact concept from the wording; the first correct identification saves most time.

Formula shortcut

Write only the necessary formula for Events; avoid using long methods when an option-based shortcut works.

Memory method

Remember Favourable/total trick as the first shortcut for this topic.

Pattern recognition trick

Look for repeated structures, ratios, signs, keywords, and option patterns in Events.

Option elimination trick

Eliminate impossible options using parity, magnitude, grammar sense, or boundary values.

Common trap

The usual trap in Events is using the wrong base, wrong unit, wrong order, or assuming extra information.

30-second solving method

Convert the question into one line of formula or one small diagram, then test the closest option.

Fast solving method for TCS NQT

Use Favourable/total trick, Order matters trick and avoid lengthy manual expansion.

Solved Examples

Example 1: Probability of getting even number on a die.
Even outcomes = 2,4,6 = 3. Total = 6. Probability = 3/6 = 1/2.
Shortcut: Favourable/total.
Example 2: AP: a=5, d=3, find 6th term.
T6 = 5+(6-1)×3 = 20.
Shortcut: Use nth term formula.

Common Mistakes

  • Starting Events without identifying the exact concept.
  • Copying numbers incorrectly from the question.
  • Selecting the first matching option without checking all conditions.
  • Ignoring units, signs, order, tense, or base value.
  • Not reviewing wrong answers after practice.
Simple probability

Simple probability measures chance. Count favourable outcomes and total possible outcomes carefully. For counting-based probability, decide whether order matters before using permutation or combination.

  1. Step 1: Read the question and identify that it belongs to Simple probability.
  2. Step 2: Write the given values or conditions in short form.
  3. Step 3: Choose the correct rule, formula, or logic pattern.
  4. Step 4: Solve neatly and compare with options.
  5. Step 5: Recheck units, signs, and hidden conditions before final answer.

Tricks to Remember

Concept trick

Before solving Simple probability, identify the exact concept from the wording; the first correct identification saves most time.

Formula shortcut

Write only the necessary formula for Simple probability; avoid using long methods when an option-based shortcut works.

Memory method

Remember Favourable/total trick as the first shortcut for this topic.

Pattern recognition trick

Look for repeated structures, ratios, signs, keywords, and option patterns in Simple probability.

Option elimination trick

Eliminate impossible options using parity, magnitude, grammar sense, or boundary values.

Common trap

The usual trap in Simple probability is using the wrong base, wrong unit, wrong order, or assuming extra information.

30-second solving method

Convert the question into one line of formula or one small diagram, then test the closest option.

Fast solving method for TCS NQT

Use Favourable/total trick, Order matters trick and avoid lengthy manual expansion.

Solved Examples

Example 1: Probability of getting even number on a die.
Even outcomes = 2,4,6 = 3. Total = 6. Probability = 3/6 = 1/2.
Shortcut: Favourable/total.
Example 2: AP: a=5, d=3, find 6th term.
T6 = 5+(6-1)×3 = 20.
Shortcut: Use nth term formula.

Common Mistakes

  • Starting Simple probability without identifying the exact concept.
  • Copying numbers incorrectly from the question.
  • Selecting the first matching option without checking all conditions.
  • Ignoring units, signs, order, tense, or base value.
  • Not reviewing wrong answers after practice.
